coredata.dc:title: Construction of a pH-responsive drug delivery platform based on the hybrid of mesoporous silica and chitosan

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Keliang
Gao, Jun
Li, Shangji
Ma, Ting
Deng, Linhong
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) have been widely used for drug delivery due to their large specific surface area and excellent biocompatibility. However, the mesoporous structure of MSN would lead to the inevitable “premature release” of the drugs, and therefore the modification of MSN for controlled delivery seems to be a necessary step. Herein, chitosan (CS) was used for the surface functionalization of MSN via amidation reaction, and the introduced CS could function as a “gatekeeper” and the drug of methotrexate (MTX) might be encapsulated in the mesopores of MSN. As a result, the “premature release” of the encapsulated MTX could be effectively circumvented with the aid of the CS cap. More importantly, the drug delivery from the hybrid of MSN and CS (MSN/CS) can be endowed with pH-sensitivity by the introduction of CS because the amide bonding between CS and MSN is highly pH-sensitive. The cumulative release of MTX from the MSN/CS is more pronounced at pH 5.0 (80.86%) than those at pH 6.8 (40.46%) and pH 7.4 (18.25%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Chitosan
pH-sensitivity
Drug delivery
Methotrexate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Encapsulation of MTX into the hybrid of MSN/CS
pH-responsive delivery of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS
Encapsulation of MTX into the MSN/CS hybrid and pH-responsive delivery of MTX
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS
“Gatekeeper” role of CS layer in the MTX/MSN/CS
pH-Responsive delivery of MTX from MTX/MSN/CS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation of MTX/MSN/CS and pH-responsive delivery of MTX from the MSN/CS hybrid.
FT-IR spectra of NH2-MSN before reflux, NH2-MSN and COOH-MSN.
FESEM and TEM images of MTX/COOH-MSN (A, C) and MTX/MSN/CS (B, D).
Determined zeta potentials of NH2-MSN, COOH-MSN, MTX, MTX/COOH-MSN, CS and MTX/MSN/CS.
Nitrogen adsorption (black line) and desorption (red line) isotherms of COOH-MSN (A) and MTX/MSN/CS (C). Pore size distributions of COOH-MSN (B) and MTX/MSN/CS (D).
Kinetic release curves of MTX from 0.35 g of MTX/COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS in 50 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0).
Kinetic release curves of MTX from 0.6 g of MTX/MSN/CS in 50 mL 0.1 M PBS of different pH values (5.0, 6.8 and 7.4).

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of King Saud University.

head.ce:title: Construction of a pH-responsive drug delivery platform based on the hybrid of mesoporous silica and chitosan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) have been widely used for drug delivery due to their large specific surface area and excellent biocompatibility. However, the mesoporous structure of MSN would lead to the inevitable “premature release” of the drugs, and therefore the modification of MSN for controlled delivery seems to be a necessary step. Herein, chitosan (CS) was used for the surface functionalization of MSN via amidation reaction, and the introduced CS could function as a “gatekeeper” and the drug of methotrexate (MTX) might be encapsulated in the mesopores of MSN. As a result, the “premature release” of the encapsulated MTX could be effectively circumvented with the aid of the CS cap. More importantly, the drug delivery from the hybrid of MSN and CS (MSN/CS) can be endowed with pH-sensitivity by the introduction of CS because the amide bonding between CS and MSN is highly pH-sensitive. The cumulative release of MTX from the MSN/CS is more pronounced at pH 5.0 (80.86%) than those at pH 6.8 (40.46%) and pH 7.4 (18.25%).

ce:section.ce:para: The past decades have witnessed the development of mesoporous materials for controlled delivery because of their unique features such as high surface areas, large pore volume and proper pore size distributions [1–3]. Among the commonly used mesoporous materials for controlled delivery, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) are especially attractive owing to their excellent biocompatibility [4–7] and easy surface functionalization through the attachment or incorporation of organic molecules [1,8]. Although the mesopores of MSN are favor for the accommodation of drug molecules, “premature release” of drug often takes place due to the leaking of the encapsulated drug molecules through the mesopores [9,10]. Therefore, the capping of the mesopores of MSN via surface modification is considered as a necessary step and has attracted more and more attention of chemists.

Chitosan (CS) is a chitin-derived natural polysaccharide, which is considered as a potential pharmaceutical material due to its low toxicity, good biocompatibility and biodegradability and antimicrobial activities [11–13]. In addition, the abundant amino (–NH2) groups of CS make it a promising candidate used for surface modification. More importantly, CS is a pH-sensitive biopolymer, and the protonation of the –NH2 groups will lead to the dissolution of CS at pH < 6.5 (the dissociation constant (pK a) of CS [14]). As a result, CS might be an ideal material for pH-responsive gating of microporous or mesoporous structure, which is especially advantageous for microporous or mesoporous materials based controlled drug delivery.

In this work, MSN and CS are successfully integrated for the construction of a pH-responsive drug delivery platform. Aminated MSN (NH2-MSN) is first prepared by surfactant-templating method [15], which is then functionalized with carboxyl (–COOH) groups in the presence of glutaric acid. Methotrexate (MTX) which is used as an anti-cancer drug is loaded into the mesopores of the –COOH functionalized MSN (COOH-MSN). To achieve the encapsulation of MTX in the mesopores of the COOH-MSN and circumvent the “premature release” of the drug, CS is introduced to the surface of COOH-MSN via the amidation reaction between the –NH2 groups of CS and the –COOH groups of COOH-MSN. The release rate of MTX from the hybrid of MSN and CS (MSN/CS) is greatly lower than that from the COOH-MSN during the same time period. That is to say, the introduced CS cap can function as a “gatekeeper” and partially inhibit the “premature release” of the encapsulated drug. More importantly, the delivery of MTX from the developed drug delivery platform can be endowed with pH-sensitivity since the amide linkage between CS and MSN is highly pH-sensitive, and the cumulative release of MTX from the MSN/CS is more pronounced in acidic medium than that in neutral medium.

Chitosan (CS), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), absolute alcohol, methanol and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 12 M) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methotrexate (MTX), glutaric acid, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methotrexate (MTX), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were received from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of different samples were recorded with a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphologies of different samples were viewed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, model: JEM2100) (JEOL, Japan) and a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, model: Supra55) (Zeiss, Germany), respectively. The size distributions and the zeta potentials of different samples were acquired by using a laser particle size analyzer (model: ZEN3600) (Malvern, UK). The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms as well as the pore size distributions of COOH-MSN and MTX encapsulated MSN/CS (MTX/MSN/CS) were recorded on a specific surface area and pore size analyzer (model: ASAP2010) (Micromeritics, USA). The concentration of MTX was monitored by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (model: U-3900) (Hitachi, Japan), and the encapsulation ratio of MTX in the developed MTX/MSN/CS and the cumulative release of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS at time t could be calculated according to the determined concentrations of the residual MTX.

NH2-MSN was prepared by a traditional surfactant-templating method [15]. In a typical procedure, 0.5 g of CTAB was added into the mixture of NH3·H2O (0.9 mL), absolute alcohol (60 mL) and deionized water (80 mL) and continuously stirred for 30 min. Next, 0.9 mL of TEOS and 1.1 mL of APTES were successively added dropwise to the mixture with continuous stirring. After another 6 h, the products were centrifuged, washed thoroughly with deionized water and absolute alcohol, and dried. The obtained solids were refluxed in the mixture of methanol (90 mL) and concentrated HCl (12 M, 10 mL) for 24 h to remove the CTAB template, and the operation was repeated three times for the complete removal of CTAB. Finally, the products were centrifugally collected and dried, which were referred to as NH2-MSN.

The COOH-MSN was prepared according to the method previously reported [16] with slight modification. Briefly, 0.5 g of glutaric acid was dissolved in 1.0 mL of DMF under stirring, and then 1.2 g of NH2-MSN and 16 mL of absolute alcohol were added into the mixture and sonicated for 30 min. Next, the mixture was continuously stirred for another 6 h at room temperature, and the obtained products referred to as COOH-MSN were centrifugally collected, washed with hot absolute alcohol for four times, and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of MTX into the hybrid of MSN/CS
ce:section.ce:para: 0.6 g of the as-prepared COOH-MSN was added into 50 mL of the aqueous solution of MTX (60 µg mL−1) with continuous stirring at 37 °C for the loading of MTX. The concentration of the residual MTX in the solution was monitored by the UV spectra of MTX at the wavelength of 302 nm [6]. No further decrease in the absorbance was observed after 24 h, and therefore the operation of MTX loading was carried out for 24 h. Next, the MTX loaded COOH-MSN (MTX/COOH-MSN) was centrifugally separated and dried at ~40 °C in an oven. After that, 1.0 g of the obtained MTX/COOH-MSN was dispersed into 100 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1 M, pH 5.5) under sonication, and then 0.58 g of NHS and 0.96 g of EDC·HCl were added into this dispersion and continuously stirred for 45 min. After that, 0.4 g of CS was added into the mixture and magnetically stirred for another 12 h for the encapsulation of MTX. Finally, the dispersion was freeze-dried at −45 °C for 24 h, and the MTX encapsulated MSN/CS (MTX/MSN/CS) was obtained. According to the following equation: encapsulation ratio (%) = (mass of MTX encapsulated/mass of MTX added) × 100, the encapsulation ratio of MTX in the MSN/CS hybrid was calculated to be 97.93%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-responsive delivery of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS
ce:section.ce:para: Three samples of the as-prepared MTX/MSN/CS (0.6 g) were placed into three dialysis bags (molecular weight cut-off, 3500 Da), which were then immersed into 50 mL of 0.1 M PBS of different pH values (5.0, 6.8 and 7.4), respectively. The in vitro delivery of MTX was carried out at 37 °C under stirring. To calculate the cumulative release of MTX from the MSN/CS hybrid at different pH values, 5.0 mL of the PBS was taken out every 1 h for UV analysis to determine the released amount of MTX, meanwhile, an equal amount (5.0 mL) of fresh PBS was added into the solution for replenishment. The cumulative release of MTX at time t can be obtained by dividing the total amount of MTX released at time t by the total amount of MTX encapsulated in the MSN/CS hybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation of MTX into the MSN/CS hybrid and pH-responsive delivery of MTX
ce:section.ce:para: The COOH groups can be introduced to the surface of MSN after the surface functionalization of NH2-MSN with glutaric acid, which can be served as the anchoring sites for the further introduction of CS through amidation reaction. The introduced CS layer can act as a competent “gatekeeper”, achieving the encapsulation of MTX into the mesopores of MSN. More importantly, the amide linkage between CS and MSN is highly pH-sensitive, and therefore the delivery of MTX from the MSN/CS hybrid should be pH-responsive. The procedures showing the preparation of MTX/MSN/CS and the pH-responsive delivery of MTX from the MSN/CS hybrid are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The FT-IR spectra of different samples are shown in Fig. 2 . For the NH2-MSN before reflux, the two strong peaks at 3450 and 1087 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of SiOH and SiOSi, respectively, and the small peak at 1420 cm−1 is due to the bending vibrations of ammonium ion N+−H [3]. Another sharp peak at 2800 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of CH2 of the CTAB templates [17]. Noted that the characteristic peak of CTAB (2800 cm−1) almost disappears completely after the reflux in the mixture of methanol and concentrated HCl, suggesting that the CTAB templates are removed completely by reflux. After the surface functionalization of NH2-MSN with glutaric acid, an additional small peak is observed at 1720 cm−1 on the spectrum of COOH-MSN, which can be assigned to the CO stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group [18], and this result indicates that the COOH-MSN is successfully prepared.

The zeta potentials of different samples including NH2-MSN, COOH-MSN, MTX, MTX/COOH-MSN, CS and MTX/MSN/CS are determined at pH 5.5, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The NH2-MSN exhibits a positive zeta potential of +11.8 mV, which might be attributed to the protonation of the NH2 groups on the surface of MSN [19]. On the contrary, the COOH-MSN exhibits a negative zeta potential of –6.2 mV due to the introduction of COOH groups with high electronegativity with the aid of glutaric acid. Similarly, MTX also exhibits a negative zeta potential of −8.4 mV owing to the existence of two highly electronegative COOH groups within one MTX molecule [20], as shown in Fig. 6 . After the loading of MTX into the mesopores of MSN, the zeta potential of the MTX/COOH-MSN is further increased to −12.3 mV, which could be ascribed to the charge superposition of MTX and COOH-MSN. A positive zeta potential of +13.5 mV is observed for the CS due to the protonation of the NH2 groups on the CS chains at pH 5.5. After the introduction of the CS layer to the surface of MTX/COOH-MSN by amidation reaction, the zeta potential of the resultant MTX/MSN/CS is remarkably decreased to +2.9 mV. The decreased zeta potential of the MTX/MSN/CS might be attributed to the fact that the integration of the positively charged CS onto the surface of the negatively charged COOH-MSN through amide linkage would result in partial counteraction of the positive charge of the CS layer. The results of zeta potentials of different samples further confirm the successful introduction of CS to the surface of the MTX/COOH-MSN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS
ce:section.ce:para: Physical gas adsorption is a straightforward-to-interpret technique for the characterization of micro- and mesoporous materials [21], and therefore the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms as well as the pore size distributions of COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS are shown in Fig. 7 . A typical Type IV isotherm is observed for the two samples (Fig. 7A and C), and the hysteresis loop in the isotherms of the two samples indicates the occurrence of capillary condensation [22]. Both COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS present a narrow pore size distribution (Fig. 7B and D), and the pore diameters of the two samples determined by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method are ~3.7 nm, which is characteristic of mesoporous materials (2–50 nm). Noted that the pore diameter of COOH-MSN (~3.7 nm) is remarkably larger than the longitudinal molecular length of MTX (2.12 nm [23]), and thus the MTX molecules can be easily loaded into the mesopores of the COOH-MSN. In addition, the BET surface area and the pore volume of the COOH-MSN are 203.61 m2 g−1 and 0.084 cm3 g−1, respectively, which are significantly higher than those of the MTX/MSN/CS (BET surface: 71.52 m2 g−1; pore volume: 0.023 cm3 g−1). Compared with COOH-MSN, the greatly decreased BET surface area and pore volume of the MTX/MSN/CS might be due to the introduction of the CS coating layer onto the surface of MSN via amidation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: “Gatekeeper” role of CS layer in the MTX/MSN/CS
ce:section.ce:para: To get a deep insight into the key role of the CS layer, the kinetic release curves of MTX from 0.35 g of MTX/COOH-MSN and MTX/MSN/CS in 50 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 5.0) are recorded. As shown in Fig. 8 , the release rate of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS is significantly lower than that from the MTX/COOH-MSN. It is noteworthy that the cumulative release of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS at the 24th hour is 44.6%, which is greatly smaller than that from the MTX/COOH-MSN (61.9%) during the same time period. Obviously, the “premature release” of the loaded MTX can be effectively inhibited with the aid of the CS layer. That is to say, the introduced CS layer can function as a competent “gatekeeper” and partially inhibit the “premature release” of MTX from the mesopores of the MSN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-Responsive delivery of MTX from MTX/MSN/CS
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that amide bonding is highly pH-sensitive, i.e., amide bonding can be easily hydrolyzed in acidic medium. In this work, the CS layer is grafted to the surface of the COOH-MSN through amide linkage, and therefore it can be concluded that the delivery of MTX from the developed drug carrier should be pH-sensitive because of the hydrolysis of the amide linkage and the consequent departure of the CS layer from the carrier. The kinetic release curves of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS (0.6 g) in 0.1 M PBS of different pH values (5.0, 6.8 and 7.4) are shown in Fig. 9 . At pH 5.0, a burst release of MTX is observed in the first five hours, and the cumulative release of MTX can reach as high as 80.86% at the 24th hour. Excitingly, the burst release of MTX is greatly inhibited at pH 6.8 and 7.4 especially at pH 7.4, and the cumulative release of MTX is only 18.25% during the same time period at pH 7.4. Obviously, the pronounced delivery of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS in acidic medium (pH 5.0) can be attributed to the hydrolysis of the amide linkage between CS and MSN. As the result of hydrolysis, the CS layer which acts as the “gatekeeper” is prone to be departed from the surface of MSN, leading to the burst release of the encapsulated MTX from the carrier, as shown in Fig. 1. In fact, the pH-responsive delivery of MTX is beneficial for cancer patients, because the extracellular pH in cancer cells is generally lower than that in healthy cells (~5.7–6.8 vs. ~7.4) due to the increased production of lactic acid in cancer cells [24,25].

In summary, a pH-responsive drug delivery platform based on the hybrid of MSN and CS is facilely constructed, which is fully characterized by TEM, FESEM and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. The analysis of zeta potentials also reveals the successful integration of CS with MSN. The CS layer grafted on the surface of MSN through amidation reaction can act as a competent “gatekeeper” and greatly inhibit the “premature release” of the encapsulated MTX. Due to the high pH-sensitivity of the amide linkage between MSN and CS, the pH-responsive delivery of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS carrier is achieved. As the result of hydrolysis of the amide linkage and the consequent departure of the CS layer from the carrier, the cumulative release of MTX from the MTX/MSN/CS at pH 5.0 (80.86%) is significantly higher those at pH 6.8 (40.46%) and pH 7.4 (18.25%). These findings are especially beneficial for cancer patients since the extracellular pH in cancer cells is generally lower than that in healthy cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11532003, 31670950), Primary Research & Developement Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019653) and Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center (ACGM2016-06-27).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Core-shell structured polypyrrole/mesoporous SiO2 nanocomposite capped with graphene quantum dots as gatekeeper for irradiation-controlled release of methotrexate

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biotin-avidin as a protease-responsive cap system for controlled guest release from colloidal mesoporous silica

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. George, T.E. Abraham, Polyionic hydrocolloids for the intestinal delivery of protein drugs: Alginate and chitosan – a review, J. Controlled Release 114 (2006) 1–14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Well-dispersed chitosan-graphene quantum dots nanocomposites for electrochemical sensing platform

sb:title.sb:maintitle: tLyP-1-conjugated core-shell nanoparticles, Fe3O4NPs@mSiO2, for tumor-targeted drug delivery
